testTitle: IELTS Academic Practice Test - Complete Edition

listening.title: Listening
listening.description: You will hear a number of different recordings and you will have to answer questions on what you hear. There will be time for you to read the instructions and questions and you will have a chance to check your work. All the recordings will be played once only.

parts.title: Conversation between two people in an everyday social context

questions.question: What is the man's name?
What dates does the man want to book?
What type of room does the man prefer?
How much does the room cost per night?
What is included in the room rate?
What is the man's phone number?
How many restaurants does the woman recommend?
What is the name of the Italian restaurant?
What type of restaurant is The Harbour?
How far is Bella Vista from the hotel?

parts.title: Monologue in an everyday social context

questions.question: When was the museum established?
How many artifacts does the museum house?
What is the most popular exhibition?
Where is the Ancient Civilizations exhibition located?
What time does the Ancient Civilizations exhibition close?
What is on the first floor?
How often do guided tours start?
How much does an audio guide cost?
What time does the café close?
How many books are in the library?

parts.title: Conversation between up to four people in an educational or training context

questions.question: What is the main topic of the discussion?
What is the main advantage of solar energy?
What is the main disadvantage of solar energy?
Where is wind energy particularly effective?
What is a challenge with wind energy?
How does hydroelectric power work?
options: Uses flowing water to generate electricity
Uses solar energy to generate electricity

questions.question: What is an environmental impact of hydroelectric power?
What can large dams affect?
What is the main challenge for renewable energy?
What do we need for wind energy?
What percentage of the Earth's surface is covered by oceans?
What percentage of the world's oceans have been explored?
How deep does the photic zone extend?
What percentage of marine species do coral reefs support?
What depth marks the beginning of the deep sea?
What adaptation do many deep-sea creatures have?
When was a new species of whale discovered?
What percentage of the world's oxygen do marine plants produce?
What is causing coral reef bleaching?
What is the most important challenge for marine biologists?

reading.title: Reading
reading.description: The Reading test consists of 3 passages with a variety of question types. You will read texts taken from books, journals, magazines and newspapers.

passages.title: The History of Coffee
passages.content:
Coffee is one of the most popular beverages in the world, with over 2.25 billion cups consumed daily. The history of coffee dates back to the 9th century, when it was first discovered in Ethiopia. According to legend, a goat herder named Kaldi noticed that his goats became more energetic after eating berries from a certain tree. He tried the berries himself and experienced the same stimulating effect.

The practice of brewing coffee began in the 15th century in Yemen, where coffee beans were first roasted and brewed. From there, coffee spread to the rest of the Middle East, Persia, Turkey, and North Africa. By the 16th century, coffee had reached Europe, where it quickly became popular despite initial resistance from some religious leaders.

The first coffeehouse in Europe opened in Venice in 1645, and by the 17th century, coffeehouses had become centers of social activity and intellectual discussion throughout Europe. These establishments were often called 'penny universities' because for the price of a penny, one could purchase a cup of coffee and engage in stimulating conversation.

Coffee cultivation began in the Americas in the 18th century, starting in the Caribbean and later spreading to Central and South America. Today, Brazil is the world's largest coffee producer, followed by Vietnam and Colombia. The coffee industry employs millions of people worldwide and is a significant source of income for many developing countries.

Modern coffee culture has evolved significantly, with specialty coffee shops, artisanal roasters, and innovative brewing methods becoming increasingly popular. The third wave of coffee, which emphasizes high-quality beans, precise brewing techniques, and direct trade relationships with farmers, has transformed how people think about and consume coffee.

questions.question: When was coffee first discovered?
Where was coffee first discovered?
What was the name of the goat herder in the legend?
When did coffee brewing begin?
Where did coffee brewing first begin?
When did the first European coffeehouse open?
Where did the first European coffeehouse open?
What were coffeehouses called in Europe?
When did coffee cultivation begin in the Americas?
Which country is the world's largest coffee producer?
What is the second largest coffee producer?
What is the third largest coffee producer?
What is the third wave of coffee?
options: Emphasis on high-quality beans and precise brewing

passages.title: Climate Change and Its Effects
passages.content:
Climate change is one of the most pressing issues of our time, affecting every aspect of human life and the natural world. The primary cause of climate change is the increase in greenhouse gases in the atmosphere, particularly carbon dioxide, which has risen by more than 40% since the Industrial Revolution.

The effects of climate change are already visible around the world. Rising global temperatures have led to the melting of polar ice caps and glaciers, causing sea levels to rise. This poses a significant threat to coastal communities and island nations. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) predicts that sea levels could rise by up to one meter by the end of the century if current trends continue.

Extreme weather events are becoming more frequent and intense due to climate change. Hurricanes, droughts, floods, and heatwaves are occurring with greater frequency, causing billions of dollars in damage and displacing millions of people. The 2020 Atlantic hurricane season was the most active on record, with 30 named storms.

Climate change also affects biodiversity and ecosystems. Many species are struggling to adapt to changing temperatures and habitats. Coral reefs, which are among the most biodiverse ecosystems on Earth, are particularly vulnerable to rising ocean temperatures and acidification. The Great Barrier Reef has experienced multiple mass bleaching events in recent years.

Agriculture and food security are also at risk. Changes in temperature and precipitation patterns affect crop yields and growing seasons. Some regions may become too hot or dry for certain crops, while others may experience increased flooding. This could lead to food shortages and higher food prices globally.

Despite these challenges, there are reasons for optimism. Renewable energy technologies have become increasingly cost-effective, and many countries are committing to ambitious climate goals. The Paris Agreement, signed by 195 countries, aims to limit global warming to well below 2 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels.

Individual actions also matter. Reducing energy consumption, using public transportation, eating less meat, and supporting sustainable businesses can all contribute to reducing greenhouse gas emissions. Education and awareness about climate change are crucial for building public support for necessary policy changes.

questions.question: What is the primary cause of climate change?
How much has carbon dioxide increased since the Industrial Revolution?
What is causing sea levels to rise?
options: Melting of polar ice caps and glaciers

questions.question: How much could sea levels rise by the end of the century?
How many named storms were there in the 2020 Atlantic hurricane season?
What makes coral reefs particularly vulnerable?
What has the Great Barrier Reef experienced?
How does climate change affect agriculture?
options: Changes in temperature and precipitation patterns

questions.question: What could lead to food shortages?
options: Changes in crop yields and growing seasons

questions.question: What is the Paris Agreement's goal?
options: Limit global warming to well below 2 degrees Celsius

questions.question: How many countries signed the Paris Agreement?
What can individuals do to help?
options: Reduce energy consumption and use public transportation

questions.question: What is crucial for building public support?
options: Education and awareness about climate change

passages.title: The Future of Artificial Intelligence
passages.content:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming the way we live, work, and interact with technology. From virtual assistants to autonomous vehicles, AI systems are becoming increasingly sophisticated and integrated into our daily lives. However, the rapid advancement of AI also raises important questions about its impact on society, employment, and human values.

Machine learning, a subset of AI, has made significant breakthroughs in recent years. Deep learning algorithms can now recognize images, understand natural language, and even generate creative content. These advances have led to practical applications in healthcare, finance, transportation, and entertainment.

In healthcare, AI is being used to diagnose diseases, develop new drugs, and personalize treatment plans. Machine learning algorithms can analyze medical images with accuracy comparable to or exceeding that of human doctors. This has the potential to improve patient outcomes and reduce healthcare costs.

The financial sector has embraced AI for fraud detection, algorithmic trading, and risk assessment. AI systems can process vast amounts of data in real-time, identifying patterns and anomalies that would be impossible for humans to detect manually. This has made financial transactions more secure and efficient.

Transportation is another area where AI is making significant strides. Autonomous vehicles promise to reduce traffic accidents, improve fuel efficiency, and provide mobility for people who cannot drive. However, the widespread adoption of self-driving cars also raises concerns about job displacement and safety.

Despite these benefits, AI also presents challenges. One major concern is the potential for job displacement as AI systems become capable of performing tasks traditionally done by humans. Some estimates suggest that up to 40% of jobs could be automated in the coming decades.

Another concern is the issue of bias in AI systems. Machine learning algorithms learn from data, and if that data contains biases, the AI system will perpetuate those biases. This can lead to unfair treatment of certain groups of people in areas such as hiring, lending, and criminal justice.

Privacy is also a significant concern. AI systems often require large amounts of personal data to function effectively. This raises questions about how this data is collected, stored, and used, and whether individuals have sufficient control over their personal information.

To address these challenges, researchers and policymakers are working on developing ethical AI frameworks and regulations. These efforts aim to ensure that AI is developed and deployed in ways that benefit society while minimizing potential harms. The goal is to create AI systems that are not only intelligent but also fair, transparent, and accountable.

questions.question: What is machine learning?
What can deep learning algorithms do?
options: Recognize images and understand natural language

questions.question: In healthcare, what can AI do?
options: Diagnose diseases and develop new drugs

questions.question: How accurate can AI be in analyzing medical images?
options: Comparable to or exceeding human doctors

questions.question: In finance, what is AI used for?
What do autonomous vehicles promise?
options: Reduce traffic accidents and improve fuel efficiency

questions.question: What percentage of jobs could be automated?
What is a major concern about AI systems?
What can lead to bias in AI systems?
Where can AI bias lead to unfair treatment?
What is a significant concern about AI?
What are researchers working on?
What is the goal for AI systems?
options: To be fair, transparent, and accountable

questions.question: What do AI systems often require?

writing.title: Writing
writing.description: The Writing test consists of 2 tasks. Task 1 requires you to describe visual information in at least 150 words. Task 2 requires you to write an essay in response to a point of view, argument or problem in at least 250 words.

tasks.title: Task 1: Academic Writing
tasks.instruction: The chart below shows the percentage of households in owned and rented accommodation in England and Wales between 1918 and 2011. Summarize the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

tasks.title: Task 2: Essay Writing
tasks.instruction: Some people believe that technology has made our lives more complicated, while others argue that it has simplified our daily routines. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

speaking.title: Speaking
speaking.description: The Speaking test is a face-to-face interview with an examiner. It consists of 3 parts: Part 1 (Introduction and interview), Part 2 (Individual long turn), and Part 3 (Two-way discussion).

parts.title: Introduction and Interview
parts.description: The examiner will ask you general questions about yourself and familiar topics such as home, family, work, studies and interests.
sampleQuestions: What do you like about your job/studies?
What do you do in your free time?

parts.title: Individual Long Turn
parts.description: You will be given a task card with a topic. You have 1 minute to prepare and then speak for 1-2 minutes on the topic.

taskCard.topic: Describe a memorable trip you have taken.
points: and explain why this trip was memorable for you

parts.title: Two-way Discussion
parts.description: The examiner will ask you further questions connected to the topic in Part 2. These questions are more abstract and require you to discuss, analyze and speculate.
sampleQuestions: How has tourism changed in your country over the past decade?
What are the benefits and drawbacks of traveling alone?
Do you think technology has made travel easier or more complicated?
How important is it for young people to travel?

metadata.author: IELTS Practice Test System
metadata.description: Complete IELTS Academic Practice Test with all four sections

breakdown.speaking: 20 points (3 parts, based on content quality)

bandScores.description: IELTS Band Scores are calculated based on raw scores and converted to the 9-band scale
bandScores.reading: Based on Academic Reading IELTS standard
bandScores.writing: Based on task completion and word count
bandScores.speaking: Based on fluency, coherence, lexical resource, grammar, and pronunciation
